questions.question: A mill fully kills a heat of steel with silicon and aluminum before teeming. Compared with a rimmed practice, the ingots will typically show:
options: More blowholes but less segregation
Fewer blowholes but a deeper shrinkage pipe — which is why killed ingots get insulated hot tops
No discontinuities of any kind
A thicker chill zone and no columnar grains
questions.explanation: Killing removes the dissolved oxygen that would otherwise evolve as carbon monoxide blowholes, but the quiet freeze concentrates shrinkage into a deeper pipe — countered by hot-topping and generous cropping.

questions.question: Hot tears in an ingot form:
options: Along the weak boundaries between columnar grains, from thermal stress during cooling
Only at the ingot surface, from mold contact
In the chill zone, from rapid solidification
At the bottom center, from teeming turbulence
questions.explanation: Uneven cooling stresses the ingot while it is still hot and weak; the metal tears internally along the columnar grain boundaries, which are the weakest planes in the structure.

questions.question: Straight-beam practices for cast and heavy wrought stock monitor back-reflection amplitude as well as discrete echoes because:
options: The back reflection calibrates the screen range automatically
Some discontinuities — fine porosity, segregation-related scattering — rob energy from the back wall without returning one clean echo of their own
Back-reflection loss measures the exact depth of each flaw
Codes forbid evaluating discrete echoes in castings
questions.explanation: Distributed scatterers smaller than the beam may never paint a distinct flaw echo, but their cumulative scattering and absorption visibly pull down the back reflection — making it a sensitive detector for what discrete-echo evaluation misses.

questions.question: Stringers in rolled plate are best described as:
options: Surface cracks opened by the finishing stand
Elongated nonmetallic inclusions strung out in the rolling direction, often in mid-thickness colonies that degrade through-thickness properties
Bands of coarse grain left by uneven reheating
Gas pockets that expand into blisters during service
questions.explanation: Rolling smears inclusion clusters into wire-like stringers aligned with the rolling direction; they pepper the screen with small echoes and set up the lamellar-tearing risk under welded restraint.

questions.question: Scanning 50 mm plate with a straight beam, you see echoes at 25, 50, and 75 mm screen positions while the normal back-wall multiples at 50 and 100 mm collapse. The interpretation is:
options: A lamination at about 25 mm depth — mid-thickness — producing its own multiple-echo train
A doubling of plate thickness at that location
Normal stringer noise in sound plate
An angle-beam mode conversion at 25 mm
questions.explanation: A mid-thickness lamination reflects from 25 mm and rings its own multiples at 25 mm intervals while shadowing the true back wall — the halved spacing identifies the lamination and its depth.

questions.question: Under common plate-scanning specifications, standard coverage and the usual tightening steps are:
options: A single diagonal pass, tightened by scanning faster
Grid lines on roughly 225 mm (9 in.) centers or continuous paths, tightened to 100% coverage and mandatory edge-zone scanning for higher quality classes
Spot checks at plate corners only
Angle-beam scanning of the full surface, tightened by adding straight beam at the corners
questions.explanation: The classic pattern scans along grid lines about 225 mm apart with continuous scanning to map any finding; tighter classes demand full coverage and 100% edge-zone scanning where bevels will be cut.

questions.question: Internal bursts in bar stock are caused by:
options: Hydrogen absorbed during cold drawing
Excessive reduction per pass or working at improper temperature, rupturing the central zone
Inclusions floating to the surface during rolling
Over-aggressive straightening after cooling
questions.explanation: Pushing too much deformation per pass, or working steel outside its proper temperature range, overstresses the bar center and tears it internally — producing irregular, often roughly transverse ruptures that a radial beam alone can undercall.

questions.question: In immersion testing of round bar, the probe is deliberately offset from the bar centerline in order to:
options: Avoid the initial-pulse dead zone
Generate refracted shear waves that sweep the near-surface zone for seams and laps
Double the far-wall echo amplitude
Cancel grain-scattering noise by interference
questions.explanation: Offsetting the beam makes it strike the curved surface at an oblique angle; refraction converts it to shear waves traveling in the near-surface region — the standard technique for axial surface defects a centered beam overlooks.

questions.question: The 'cold weld' condition in ERW pipe is:
options: Porosity from damp flux in the weld seam
Lack of fusion at the solid-state bond line — a vertical planar discontinuity running axially along the seam
A transverse crack from quenching after welding
Excess penetration of filler metal into the bore
questions.explanation: ERW joins forged edges without filler; if heat or pressure is inadequate the edges never bond, leaving planar lack of fusion standing vertically in the weld plane along the pipe length — broadside to a circumferential shear wave.

questions.question: Pipe with 200 mm OD and 20 mm wall is examined circumferentially with a 45-degree shear wave. Closest approach of the beam to the pipe center is R·sin θ = 100 × 0.707 ≈ 71 mm; the bore radius is 80 mm. Does the beam examine the ID?
options: No — the beam skips along the OD without touching the bore
Yes — 71 mm is less than the 80 mm bore radius, so the chord reaches the ID surface
Only if the frequency is raised above 5 MHz
Only when scanning counterclockwise
questions.explanation: The chord penetrates to within 71 mm of the center, inside the 80 mm bore radius, so it strikes the ID (t/OD = 0.10, below the ≈0.15 limit for 45°). Thicker wall on the same OD would eventually leave the bore unexamined.
